summary:
The Mongol Invasion of the Khwarazmian Empire (1219–1221 AD) was one of [[event:2225|Genghis Khan's]] most devastating campaigns, obliterating one of the largest and most powerful Islamic states of Central Asia with a speed and thoroughness that shocked the medieval world. The Khwarazmian Shah Muhammad II had made the catastrophic error of executing Mongol trade envoys and a subsequent diplomatic delegation — an act that Mongol culture considered an unforgivable provocation requiring total military response. Genghis Khan assembled his largest army and launched a campaign from multiple directions simultaneously.

The Mongol assault on the great cities of Central Asia — Samarkand, Bukhara, Urgench, Merv, Nishapur, and Herat — was conducted with calculated ferocity. Bukhara was captured and burned; Genghis Khan reportedly gathered the city's population and declared 'I am the punishment of God; if you had not committed great sins, God would not have sent a punishment like me.' Merv, which had been one of the largest cities in the world, was destroyed and its population massacred — Persian historical sources report millions killed, modern historians estimate hundreds of thousands. The great Central Asian irrigation systems that had sustained dense populations in the otherwise arid landscape were systematically destroyed, depopulating regions for generations. The Shah fled and died on a Caspian island; his son Jalal ad-Din continued resistance for years. The destruction of Khorasan and Central Asia in this campaign represented one of the greatest cultural catastrophes of the Islamic world, setting back the region's intellectual and economic development by centuries.
